description: The NeRF paper title page or abstract figure showing the core concept of neural radiance fields for novel view synthesis
teaching_value: Introduces students to the NeRF paper as a landmark work, anchoring the method in its academic context and showing the quality of synthesized novel views
when_to_show: Show at the start of a lesson on implicit neural representations or novel view synthesis to establish the paper's contribution
suggested_caption: NeRF: Representing scenes as continuous volumetric functions. (Mildenhall et al.)

description: SVG pipeline diagram showing the NeRF method: 5D input coordinates (x,y,z,θ,φ) fed into a fully-connected network, outputting volume density and view-dependent color, then composited via volume rendering into a final image
teaching_value: Visually maps the entire NeRF pipeline from input coordinates to rendered output, helping students understand how spatial position and viewing direction are combined and how the network outputs are used in volume rendering
when_to_show: Show when explaining the NeRF pipeline architecture, especially when introducing how 5D coordinates map to color and density outputs
suggested_caption: NeRF pipeline: 5D coordinates → MLP → density & color → rendered image. (Mildenhall et al.)

description: Diagram illustrating how camera rays are cast through a scene, with sample points along each ray queried as 5D coordinates (position + viewing direction) to synthesize a novel view
teaching_value: Concretely shows students how volume rendering works in NeRF — rays are cast from a camera, points are sampled along each ray, and the network is queried at each point to accumulate color and density into a pixel value
when_to_show: Show when teaching how NeRF uses volume rendering to synthesize images, particularly when explaining the connection between camera rays and network queries
suggested_caption: Camera rays sample 5D coordinates; volume rendering accumulates color per pixel. (Mildenhall et al.)

description: SVG diagram showing the fully-connected MLP network architecture used in NeRF, illustrating how spatial coordinates (x,y,z) and viewing direction (θ,φ) are input at different stages to produce volume density σ and RGB color output
teaching_value: Clarifies the two-stage network design: density is predicted from position alone (view-independent), while color is predicted from both position and viewing direction (view-dependent), teaching students why this separation matters for realistic rendering
when_to_show: Show when explaining the NeRF network architecture, especially to highlight why viewing direction is injected later in the network than spatial position
suggested_caption: NeRF MLP: position inputs density; direction inputs view-dependent color. (Mildenhall et al.)

description: Comparison image showing rays vs. conical frustums for sampling in NeRF vs. mip-NeRF, illustrating how mip-NeRF casts anti-aliased conical frustums instead of infinitesimally thin rays to reduce aliasing at multiple scales
teaching_value: Helps students understand the aliasing problem in NeRF and how mip-NeRF addresses it by representing the volume of a frustum rather than a point sample, connecting to concepts of anti-aliasing and multiscale representation
when_to_show: Show when discussing limitations of NeRF and improvements in mip-NeRF, or when teaching anti-aliasing strategies in neural rendering
suggested_caption: mip-NeRF casts conical frustums (right) vs. NeRF's thin rays (left) to reduce aliasing. (Barron et al.)

description: Diagram showing Occupancy Networks architecture: a neural network with 5 ResNet blocks taking a feature vector and 3D point as input and predicting binary occupancy (inside/outside classification) for implicit surface representation
teaching_value: Illustrates how a neural network can act as a learned binary classifier over 3D space to define implicit surfaces, helping students understand coordinate-based implicit representations as an alternative to voxels or meshes
when_to_show: When introducing implicit neural representations as precursors to NeRF; use to contrast with explicit 3D representations (voxels, meshes) before explaining signed distance functions
suggested_caption: Occupancy Networks predict binary inside/outside occupancy from 3D coordinates. (Mescheder et al., CVPR 2019)

alt: 3D Shapes generated using a GAN using IM-NET as the decoder
description: Visualization of 3D shapes generated using a GAN where IM-NET serves as the implicit decoder, showing diverse generated shape outputs from a 6-layer MLP that predicts binary occupancy given a feature vector and 3D coordinate
teaching_value: Demonstrates that an implicit MLP decoder can be used generatively (GAN-style) for shape synthesis, helping students understand how implicit functions enable downstream tasks like shape generation and auto-encoding beyond reconstruction
when_to_show: When comparing IM-NET to Occupancy Networks to show parallel development of implicit representations; useful when discussing how implicit functions enable generative modeling
suggested_caption: IM-NET's implicit MLP decoder enables GAN-based 3D shape generation. (Chen & Zhang, CVPR 2019)

alt: The Stanford bunny rendered through a learned signed distance function (SDF)
description: Rendering of the Stanford bunny reconstructed through a learned signed distance function (SDF), showing smooth, high-quality surface geometry produced by DeepSDF's 8-layer MLP with skip connections that outputs continuous signed distance values
teaching_value: Concretely shows the output quality achievable with SDF-based implicit representations, and introduces the 8-layer MLP with skip-connections architecture that directly influenced NeRF's design
when_to_show: When explaining the architectural lineage from DeepSDF to NeRF; highlight the skip-connection design as a direct precursor to NeRF's network architecture
suggested_caption: DeepSDF reconstructs the Stanford bunny via a learned signed distance function. (Park et al., CVPR 2019)

alt: PIFu regresses color and an SDF from pixel aligned features, enabling single-view reconstruction
description: Diagram illustrating PIFu's pipeline: 3D points are projected onto image feature maps to extract pixel-aligned features, which are then used to regress both color and SDF values, enabling detailed single-view human reconstruction
teaching_value: Introduces the concept of pixel-aligned feature extraction — projecting 3D points back into 2D image space — which is a key idea later reused in PixelNeRF, helping students trace the conceptual lineage of view-conditioned neural rendering
when_to_show: When bridging from implicit surface methods to NeRF extensions; specifically reference when introducing PixelNeRF to show how pixel-aligned features enable generalization across scenes
suggested_caption: PIFu projects 3D points to pixel-aligned features for single-view reconstruction. (Saito et al., ICCV 2019)

alt: In Neural Volumes, a latent code is decoded into a 3D volume, and a new image is then obtained by volume rendering
description: Diagram of the Neural Volumes pipeline: a latent code is decoded by a neural network into a 3D volume of density and color, which is then rendered into a new image via volume rendering, showing the encode-decode-render flow
teaching_value: Establishes Neural Volumes as the first true volume rendering approach for view synthesis, helping students understand the conceptual bridge between implicit representations and NeRF-style volumetric rendering
when_to_show: When transitioning from implicit surface methods to NeRF; use to show that volume rendering for view synthesis predates NeRF and to explain what NeRF improves upon
suggested_caption: Neural Volumes decodes a latent code into a 3D volume for differentiable rendering. (Facebook Reality Labs)

alt: A NeRF stores a volumetric scene representation as the weights of an MLP, trained on many images with known pose
description: Overview diagram of the NeRF setup showing: multiple input images with known camera poses used to train an MLP that stores the entire scene as network weights, mapping 3D coordinates and view direction to density and color
teaching_value: Provides the high-level conceptual framework of NeRF — scene as MLP weights, trained from posed images — helping students understand what is being learned and how the representation differs from explicit 3D structures
when_to_show: As the primary introductory diagram when first explaining NeRF; show before the pipeline figure to establish the overall learning setup before detailing the rendering procedure
suggested_caption: NeRF encodes a scene as MLP weights trained on posed images. (Mildenhall et al., ECCV 2020)

alt: New views are rendered by integrating the density and color at regular intervals along each viewing ray
description: Detailed pipeline diagram of NeRF's rendering procedure: rays are cast from a camera, 3D points are sampled along each ray, positional encoding (Fourier features) is applied, the MLP outputs density and color, and numerical integration accumulates these into a final pixel color
teaching_value: Explains the step-by-step volumetric rendering process in NeRF — ray casting, point sampling, positional encoding, MLP query, and numerical integration — giving students a mechanistic understanding of how novel views are synthesized
when_to_show: Immediately after the NeRF setup diagram; use to walk through each rendering step in detail, pausing to explain positional encoding and the volume rendering integral
suggested_caption: NeRF renders pixels by integrating density and color along viewing rays. (Mildenhall et al., ECCV 2020)

alt: Using an octree as an organizing data structure, the NSVF paper claims both Neural Volumes and NeRF as special cases. Feature embeddings on the vertices of each voxel are interpolated and fed to a (smaller) NLP outputting density and color, as in NeRF
description: Architecture diagram of Neural Sparse Voxel Fields (NSVF): a sparse octree organizes the scene, feature embeddings on voxel vertices are trilinearly interpolated for sampled 3D points, and a smaller MLP outputs density and color — shown as a generalization encompassing both Neural Volumes and NeRF
teaching_value: Illustrates how sparse voxel data structures can accelerate NeRF rendering by skipping empty space, and shows how NSVF unifies prior work; helps students understand the performance-accuracy tradeoff in neural rendering
when_to_show: When discussing NeRF performance improvements; use to explain how spatial data structures reduce the number of MLP queries needed and why this speeds up both training and rendering
suggested_caption: NSVF uses a sparse octree with vertex features to accelerate NeRF rendering. (Liu et al., NeurIPS 2020)

alt: Dynamic Neural Radiance Fields are quite similar to Nerfies in terms of task, but use a morphable face model to simplifyy training and rendering.
description: Diagram comparing Dynamic Neural Radiance Fields (DNRF) for 4D facial avatar reconstruction, showing how a deformable face model (morphable model) is integrated as a strong inductive bias into the NeRF pipeline to enable parametric control over facial dynamics
teaching_value: Demonstrates how domain-specific priors (a morphable face model) can be incorporated into NeRF to constrain the solution space for dynamic scenes, helping students understand the role of inductive bias in extending NeRF to non-static subjects
when_to_show: When covering NeRF extensions to dynamic scenes and human avatars; contrast with Nerfies' deformation MLP approach to illustrate the spectrum from data-driven to model-based priors
suggested_caption: DNRF embeds a morphable face model into NeRF for parametric 4D avatar control. (Gafni et al.)

alt: NeRD, as other methods in this section, go beyond NeRF in learning more complex local reflectance models along with density. NeRD itself also learns a global illumination model for each scene in the training set , as illustrated in panel d.
description: Multi-panel diagram of NeRD (Neural Reflectance Decomposition) showing how the method decomposes scenes into reflectance components and learns a global illumination model (spherical harmonics) per scene, with panel d specifically illustrating the illumination model across training scenes.
teaching_value: Helps students understand how NeRD extends NeRF by separating local reflectance from global illumination, making the decomposition pipeline concrete and showing how spherical harmonics encode scene lighting.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how NeRF-based methods can be extended to support relighting by disentangling geometry, reflectance, and illumination; use panel d to anchor the discussion of per-scene illumination inference.
suggested_caption: NeRD decomposes scenes into reflectance and spherical harmonics illumination per scene. (Boss et al.)

alt: GRAF (and other conditional variants of NeRF) add latent codes for shape and/or appearance, so neural volume rendering can be used in generative fashion, as well as inference.
description: Diagram illustrating GRAF's architecture, showing how latent codes for shape and appearance are injected into a conditional NeRF model, enabling generative 3D-aware image synthesis and inference via GAN-style training.
teaching_value: Clarifies how conditioning NeRF with latent codes transforms it from a scene-specific reconstruction tool into a generative model, helping students understand the bridge between neural radiance fields and generative adversarial networks.
when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from standard NeRF to generative variants; use to contrast scene-specific NeRF with latent-conditioned models that can synthesize novel instances.
suggested_caption: GRAF adds shape and appearance latent codes to NeRF for generative 3D synthesis. (Schwarz et al.)

description: Animated GIF demonstrating Object Scattering Functions (OSFs) composing a scene with objects at arbitrary positions, showing how object-centric neural representations can be repositioned within a scene while maintaining realistic appearance.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the compositional capability of OSFs — students can see that object-centric NeRF models allow free repositioning of objects, making the concept of object-centric coordinate frames tangible.
when_to_show: Show when introducing compositional neural scene rendering; use the left/position example to motivate why object-centric representations are needed over monolithic scene NeRFs.
suggested_caption: OSFs enable composing scenes with objects at arbitrary positions. (Guo et al.)

description: Animated GIF demonstrating Object Scattering Functions (OSFs) composing a scene with varying lighting conditions, showing how the object-centric neural representation supports realistic relighting of composed scenes.
teaching_value: Complements Image 3 by showing the lighting dimension of OSF compositionality — students see that the same framework handles both spatial composition and illumination variation, reinforcing the power of Monte Carlo rendering within OSFs.
when_to_show: Show immediately after Image 3 to complete the two-axis demonstration of OSF capabilities (position and lighting); useful when contrasting OSFs with simpler composition methods like GIRAFFE.
suggested_caption: OSFs support realistic relighting of composed scenes via Monte Carlo rendering. (Guo et al.)

alt: The iNERF teaser GIF: iNERF uses NeRF as a synthesis model in a pose optimizer
description: Animated GIF teaser for iNeRF showing a NeRF model being used inversely: given an observed image, a pose optimizer iteratively refines camera pose estimates by minimizing the difference between NeRF-rendered and observed images.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates the inversion concept — using a trained NeRF as a differentiable renderer inside an optimization loop for pose estimation — helping students understand how NeRF can be repurposed beyond novel view synthesis.
when_to_show: Introduce when discussing applications of NeRF beyond view synthesis, particularly pose estimation; use to show how a synthesis model can be 'inverted' via gradient-based optimization.
suggested_caption: iNeRF inverts a NeRF model to estimate camera pose via iterative rendering. (Yen-Chen et al.)
